title: Find method should be preferred instead of the FirstOrDefault extension
description: Both the List.Find method and the Enumerable.FirstOrDefault method can be used to locate the first element that meets a specified condition within a collection. However, for List objects, List.Find may offer superior performance compared to Enumerable.FirstOrDefault. While the performance difference might be negligible for small collections, it can become significant for larger collections.
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

noncompliant:
List<User> users = GetUsers();
var user = users.FirstOrDefault(u => u.Id == 5); // Noncompliant: using LINQ on List
compliant:
List<User> users = GetUsers();
var user = users.Find(u => u.Id == 5); // Compliant: using List.Find

logic.detection_conditions: Detects FirstOrDefault extension method calls on List<T> objects. List.Find should be preferred for better performance.
logic.reasoning: Detects FirstOrDefault extension method calls on List<T> objects. For List objects, List.Find offers better performance. We check CallExpression nodes for FirstOrDefault method name and verify the source contains List type indicators.

should_trigger: List<User> users = GetUsers(); var user = users.FirstOrDefault(u => u.Id == 5);
var list = new List<int>(); var item = list.FirstOrDefault(x => x > 0);
should_not_trigger: List<User> users = GetUsers(); var user = users.Find(u => u.Id == 5);
IEnumerable<int> items = GetItems(); var item = items.FirstOrDefault(x => x > 0);
